title_en: Belief in the Angels and Revelations
title_ar: Belief in the Angels and Revelations
title_id: Iman kepada Malaikat dan Kitab-Kitab yang Diturunkan
content_id:
Dua dari enam rukun iman Islam — iman kepada malaikat (al-mala'ikah) dan iman kepada kitab-kitab yang diturunkan (al-kutub) — dibahas dalam bab ini dari al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah. Keduanya bukan perkara pinggiran tetapi komponen fundamental dari pandangan dunia Islam, yang tanpanya pemahaman yang tepat tentang kenabian, wahyu, dan komunikasi ilahi tidak mungkin terwujud.

Al-Tahawi menegaskan iman kepada malaikat sebagai sekelompok makhluk yang diciptakan dari cahaya, sebagaimana ditetapkan oleh hadis Aisyah dalam Shahih Muslim: 'Malaikat diciptakan dari cahaya, jin diciptakan dari nyala api yang tak berasap, dan Adam diciptakan dari apa yang telah diceritakan kepada kalian.' Malaikat tidak memiliki kehendak yang bertentangan dengan perintah Allah — Al-Qur'an menyatakan tentang mereka: 'Mereka tidak mendahului-Nya dalam perkataan, dan mereka berbuat sesuai perintah-Nya' (QS. al-Anbiya': 27). Mereka bukan laki-laki maupun perempuan dalam pengertian manusia, tidak makan maupun minum, tidak kelelahan dalam beribadah, dan tidak memiliki kemampuan untuk berbuat dosa. Mereka yang meninggikan malaikat ke status ilahi — seperti sebagian orang Arab pagan yang mengklaim mereka sebagai putri-putri Allah — dibantah secara tegas oleh Al-Qur'an.

Malaikat-malaikat yang disebutkan namanya yang diyakini dalam doktrin Islam meliputi Jibril (Gabriel), yang menyampaikan wahyu kepada seluruh para nabi; Mikail (Michael), yang mengawasi rezeki dan hujan; Israfil, yang akan meniup Sangkakala pada Hari Kebangkitan; Izra'il atau Malak al-Mawt, Malaikat Maut yang mencabut nyawa; para pembawa 'Arsy; Munkar dan Nakir, yang menanyai orang mati di kubur; serta Ridwan, penjaga surga, dan Malik, penjaga neraka. Di luar malaikat-malaikat bernama ini, Al-Qur'an menggambarkan jumlah malaikat yang sangat banyak yang senantiasa beribadah dan bertasbih kepada Allah.

Mengenai kitab-kitab yang diturunkan, al-Tahawi menegaskan iman kepada semua kitab yang diturunkan Allah kepada para nabi-Nya, termasuk Tawrat (Taurat) yang diberikan kepada Musa, Zabur yang diberikan kepada Dawud, Injil yang diberikan kepada Isa, dan Al-Qur'an yang diberikan kepada Muhammad shallallahu 'alaihi wa sallam. Al-Qur'an sendiri bersaksi tentang kitab-kitab sebelumnya: 'Dia menurunkan kepadamu Al-Kitab dengan benar, membenarkan kitab yang sebelumnya, dan Dia telah menurunkan Taurat dan Injil sebelumnya sebagai petunjuk bagi manusia' (QS. Ali 'Imran: 3-4).

Satu poin doktrin penting yang diimplikasikan al-Tahawi, dan yang dielaborasi lebih lanjut oleh para komentator kemudian, adalah perbedaan antara iman kepada kitab-kitab asli yang diturunkan dan penerimaan terhadap teks Taurat dan Injil yang tersedia saat ini. Al-Qur'an secara tegas menyatakan bahwa kitab-kitab sebelumnya telah mengalami tahrif — baik dalam pengertian perubahan teks maupun salah interpretasi. Kaum Muslim menegaskan bahwa Musa menerima Taurat yang benar dan Isa menerima Injil yang benar dari Allah, sambil mengakui bahwa kedua kitab asli tersebut tidak lagi tersedia dalam bentuk murninya. Ini bukan kontradiksi tetapi perbedaan antara asli ilahi dan versi yang ditransmisikan oleh manusia.

Al-Qur'an, sebaliknya, dijaga melalui jaminan langsung Allah: 'Sesungguhnya Kami-lah yang menurunkan Adz-Dzikr, dan sesungguhnya Kami benar-benar memeliharanya' (QS. al-Hijr: 9). Perlindungan ini dipertahankan melalui institusi hafiz (para penghafal), pelestarian sistem sanad untuk bacaan Al-Qur'an (sepuluh qira'at kanonik), dan fakta historis bahwa Al-Qur'an sebagaimana dihafal dan ditulis hari ini identik dengan teks yang dikompilasi di bawah Utsman ibn Affan dan dikonfirmasi oleh para Sahabat.
content_en:
Two of the six pillars of Islamic faith — belief in angels (al-mala'ikah) and belief in the revealed books (al-kutub) — are addressed in this chapter of al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah. These are not peripheral matters but foundational components of the Islamic worldview without which a proper understanding of prophethood, revelation, and divine communication would be impossible.

Al-Tahawi affirms belief in the angels as a community of created beings made from light, as established by the hadith of Aishah in Sahih Muslim: 'The angels were created from light, the jinn were created from a smokeless flame of fire, and Adam was created from what has been described to you.' The angels have no independent will in opposition to Allah's commands — the Quran states of them: 'They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command' (Quran 21:27). They are neither male nor female in the human sense, neither eat nor drink, do not tire in worship, and have no capacity for sin. Those who elevated them to divine status — as some pagan Arabs did, claiming them to be daughters of Allah — are explicitly refuted by the Quran.

The named angels affirmed in Islamic doctrine include Jibril (Gabriel), who brought revelation to all the prophets; Mika'il (Michael), who oversees provisions and rain; Israfil, who will blow the Trumpet on the Day of Resurrection; Izra'il or Malak al-Mawt, the Angel of Death who takes souls; the Bearers of the Throne; Munkar and Nakir, who question the dead in the grave; and Ridwan, the keeper of Paradise, and Malik, the keeper of Hellfire. Beyond these named angels, the Quran describes vast numbers of angels engaged in perpetual worship and glorification of Allah.

Regarding the revealed books, al-Tahawi affirms belief in all the books that Allah sent down to His prophets, including the Tawrah (Torah) given to Musa, the Zabur (Psalms) given to Dawud, the Injil (Gospel) given to Isa, and the Quran given to Muhammad ﷺ. The Quran itself attests to these earlier scriptures: 'He has sent down upon you the Book in truth, confirming what was before it, and He had sent down the Tawrah and the Injil before as guidance for the people' (Quran 3:3-4).

A critical doctrinal point that al-Tahawi implies, and that later commentators elaborate, is the distinction between belief in the original revealed books and acceptance of the currently available texts of the Torah and Gospel. The Quran explicitly states that the earlier scriptures were subject to corruption (tahrif) — both in the sense of textual alteration and in the sense of misinterpretation. Muslims affirm that Musa received a true Torah and Isa received a true Gospel from Allah, while acknowledging that these originals are no longer available in their pure form. This is not a contradiction but a distinction between the divine original and the humanly transmitted version.

The Quran, by contrast, is preserved by Allah's direct guarantee: 'Indeed, it is We who sent down the Reminder, and indeed, it is We who will guard it' (Quran 15:9). This protection has been maintained through the institution of hafiz (memorizers), the preservation of the isnad system for Quranic recitation (the ten canonical qira'at), and the historical fact that the Quran as memorized and written today is identical to the text compiled under Uthman ibn Affan and confirmed by the Companions.
content_ar:
Two of the six pillars of Islamic faith — belief in angels (al-mala'ikah) and belief in the revealed books (al-kutub) — are addressed in this chapter of al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah. These are not peripheral matters but foundational components of the Islamic worldview without which a proper understanding of prophethood, revelation, and divine communication would be impossible.

Al-Tahawi affirms belief in the angels as a community of created beings made from light, as established by the hadith of Aishah in Sahih Muslim: 'The angels were created from light, the jinn were created from a smokeless flame of fire, and Adam was created from what has been described to you.' The angels have no independent will in opposition to الله's commands — the Quran states of them: 'They do not precede Him in speech, and they act by His command' (Quran 21:27). They are neither male nor female in the human sense, neither eat nor drink, do not tire in worship, and have no capacity for sin. Those who elevated them to divine status — as some pagan Arabs did, claiming them to be daughters of الله — are explicitly refuted by the Quran.

The named angels affirmed in Islamic doctrine include Jibril (Gabriel), who brought revelation to all the prophets; Mika'il (Michael), who oversees provisions and rain; Israfil, who will blow the Trumpet on the Day of Resurrection; Izra'il or Malak al-Mawt, the Angel of Death who takes souls; the Bearers of the Throne; Munkar and Nakir, who question the dead in the grave; and Ridwan, the keeper of Paradise, and Malik, the keeper of Hellfire. Beyond these named angels, the Quran describes vast numbers of angels engaged in perpetual worship and glorification of الله.

Regarding the revealed books, al-Tahawi affirms belief in all the books that الله sent down to His prophets, including the Tawrah (Torah) given to Musa, the Zabur (Psalms) given to Dawud, the Injil (Gospel) given to Isa, and the Quran given to Muhammad صلى الله عليه وسلم. The Quran itself attests to these earlier scriptures: 'He has sent down upon you the Book in truth, confirming what was before it, and He had sent down the Tawrah and the Injil before as guidance for the people' (Quran 3:3-4).

A critical doctrinal point that al-Tahawi implies, and that later commentators elaborate, is the distinction between belief in the original revealed books and acceptance of the currently available texts of the Torah and Gospel. The Quran explicitly states that the earlier scriptures were subject to corruption (tahrif) — both in the sense of textual alteration and in the sense of misinterpretation.
